title: From Fundamental Dynamics to Applied Cryptography: Studies on the Quantum Speed Limit and Fully Passive Quantum Key Distribution
abstract: This thesis studies two distinct frontiers of quantum information processing: the   fundamental physical limits of dynamical evolution and the practical realization of secure   quantum communication networks.
summary: This work provides a new mathematical framework for universal Quantum Speed Limits that applies to both closed and open quantum systems. It also introduces fully passive quantum key distribution protocols that enhance security by removing active modulators, thereby closing critical hardware-based side-channels.
main_problem: The thesis addresses two frontiers: improving the tightness and universality of Quantum Speed Limit (QSL) bounds for quantum dynamics, and mitigating side-channel vulnerabilities in QKD caused by active hardware modulators.
main_result: The author derives a new family of universal QSL bounds based on weighted l_p-seminorms that apply to both closed and open systems, and develops fully passive MDI-QKD and CKA protocols that eliminate source-side side-channels.
method: Mathematical frameworks include weighted l_p-seminorms, linear programming for decoy-state analysis, and a branch-cutting algorithm for multi-party optimization; cryptographic methods include the decoy-state method and twin-field interference.
model_or_system: The work covers quantum communication networks (MDI-QKD and multi-user CKA using twin-field interference) and quantum dynamics (closed and open systems, including Lindbladian dynamics and spontaneous emission).
key_observables: Quantum Bit Error Rate (QBER), secret key rate, photon-number yields, gain, and evolution speed.
important_parameters: Channel loss (up to 28 dB), transmission distance (approx. 140 km), photon-number statistics (Poisson distribution), and QSL parameters (norm power p, weight vector w, and basis B).
main_assumptions: The classical channel is assumed to be authenticated but public; security assumes the validity of the no-cloning theorem; and local channel components are conservatively assigned to the eavesdropper.
figures_summary: Figures show the optimized QSL for dephasing qubits and qubit coherence generation, demonstrating that the new bound is tighter and more efficient than existing specialized bounds for certain time regimes.
paper_structure: The thesis bridges fundamental dynamics and applied cryptography, progressing from the mathematical foundations of QSLs and QKD protocols to the development of new universal bounds and the implementation of fully passive, secure communication architectures.
why_interesting: The development of a unified, computationally efficient QSL framework using weighted norms is highly relevant to researchers studying the fundamental limits of open quantum systems and quantum control.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 3.2: Optical layout of the fully passive source used in my passive MDI- QKD scheme. Each user employs four independent lasers whose random phases are mixed through a network of 50:50 beam splitters (BSs) and a polarizing beam splitter (PBS), producing orthogonally polarized arms. Local classical detectors monitor the arm intensities and phases, and the resulting classical data (µH, µV , ϕH, ϕV ) are used to infer the output state parameters for basis and decoy selection [42, 60]. Figure Adapted with modifications from Fig. 1 in W. Wang et al., Phys. Rev. Lett. 130, 220801 (2023), and subsequently used in my Phys. Rev. Applied manuscript, with permission of the American Physical...

Figure 3.3: Illustration of the postselection domains used by Alice, represented in the (µH, µV , ϕ) parameter space together with their corresponding regions on the Bloch sphere. The basis-dependent partitions including the Z-basis window ∆Z and the X-basis windows ∆XY and ∆ϕ are further subdivided into several intensity bins, labeled t1, t2, t3, which define the decoy settings em- ployed in the protocol. Bob applies an identical partitioning. Figure Adapted with modifications from Fig. 2 of W. Wang et al., Phys. Rev. Lett. 130, 220801 (2023), and subsequently incorporated into my Phys. Rev. Applied manuscript, with permission of the American Physical Society.

Figure 3.4: Illustration of the small-ring sifting strategy. The key-generating regions associated with the Z basis are divided into a sequence of thin con- centric rings, indicated by the shaded segments between successive angular boundaries. Panels (a) and (b) show how these rings appear in the (µH, µV ) parameter space for Alice and Bob, while panels (c) and (d) display the cor- responding annular regions on their respective Bloch spheres. Each ring is labeled by its polar angle θ and has a differential width dθ. In the numerical evaluation of Eq. (3.8), Alice’s integration limits are marked as 0 and ∆Z, and Bob’s limits are identical. Figure reproduced from my Phys. Rev. Applied...

Figure 3.5: Simulated secret-key rates for the fully passive MDI-QKD proto- col compared with a standard actively modulated MDI-QKD scheme. Both approaches employ three decoy settings. For the passive protocol, the post- selection parameters are fixed at ∆XY = ∆ϕ = 0.2, while ∆Z is optimized over [0.01, 0.05] and the highest decoy threshold t3 is optimized in the interval [0.1, 0.99]. In the active protocol, the three decoy intensities are optimized within [0, 1]. A channel loss of 0.2 dB/km, a detector dark count probability of 10−6, and a total misalignment of 1% are assumed. The passive scheme achieves a maximum distance of approximately 143 km, with key rates that are two to three...

Figure 3.1: Schematic illustration of a measurement-device-independent quan- tum key distribution (MDI-QKD) implementation. Alice and Bob prepare phase-randomized weak coherent pulses (WCPs) in randomly chosen BB84 polarization states, created using a polarization modulator (Pol-M) and inten- sity settings controlled by a decoy-state modulator (Decoy-IM). Their optical signals are directed to an untrusted central relay, where they interfere on a 50:50 beam splitter. Each output arm is analyzed by a polarizing beam splitter (PBS) that resolves horizontal (H) and vertical (V ) polarizations, and the resulting photons are detected by four single-photon detectors.
